segments.s149-g49: بشد is an archaic/past-form verb meaning “(he) rose/went” or “it happened”; here it precedes the name (verb–subject order common in epic style) so: «بشد طوس» = “Tūs rose/started.” The phrase «پیغام کاووس برد» is a compact verb phrase: پیغام (the object) + کاووس (genitive, “of Kāvus”) + برد (past of بردن “to take/bring”), so literally “he took/carried Kāvus’s message.”
segments.s149-g50: شنیده is a perfect/perfect-participle meaning “having heard” and modifies the (implicit) subject; سخن is the noun “words/speech” (the thing heard); پیش او is a prepositional/locative phrase “in his presence / to him”; برشمرد is a past-tense verb “enumerated/recounted.” The whole clause means “having heard, he recounted the words before him.”
segments.s149-g51: بدو is the prepositional clitic “to him/unto him” (indirect object); گفت here functions as the verb “said” (word-order places indirect object before the verb); رستم is the subject (postverbal for emphasis). The conjunction/relative که begins the reported/subordinate clause: «که هر شهریار …» = “that/any king …”
segments.s149-g52: The relative-clause introduced by که modifies شهریار: که کردی مرا ناگهان خواستار = “who (you) … made me suddenly a petitioner / who suddenly desires me.” کردِی is an archaic/past-form (here used as a relative/finite verb meaning roughly “who calls/has made”); م + را is the object clitic split in the segment (م = me, را = definite/object marker) so «مرا» = “me”; ناگهان is the adverb “suddenly”; خواستار is an adjectival/noun form “desirous / one who requests.” Together: “who suddenly desires/requests me.”
segments.s149-g53: The pair گهی … گهی is a correlative “sometimes … sometimes …”. گنج بودی is second-person past “you were (a) treasure”; the second half omits a repeated بودی and reads سازِ بزم = “(the) instrument/tune (of) the banquet” (saz + ezafe understood). So the line contrasts two alternating roles: “sometimes a treasure, sometimes the music of the feast.”
segments.s149-g54: ندیدم is the negative perfect/past first-person “I did not see” (here idiomatically: “I have seen nothing”); ز is the poetic preposition “from”; کاووس is the name Kāvus; جز introduces the exception “except”; رنج رزم is a compound meaning “the suffering/hardship of battle” (رنج = suffering, رزم = battle). Literal: “I saw from Kāvus nothing except battle’s suffering.”
segments.s149-g55: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past “he commanded/ordered (said)” (often used for a king’s command). تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause “so that / that”; رخش (rokhsh) is the steed/warhorse; را is the definite object marker; زین is “saddle”; کنند is the present-subjunctive/plural “they should do / they make,” used here as the subordinate verb after تا. Together: “He commanded that they saddle his steed.”
segments.s149-g56: سواران is the subject “the horsemen/riders.” بروها functions as a participial/epithet meaning “those who go / the departing ones” (literally from برو “go”), placed in apposition to سواران; پر + از + چین + کنند is a verblike construction where پر “full” with از “of” + چین “folds/pleats” and کنند “they make/do” yields “they fill (it) full of folds.” In context: the riders who were to depart filled (the trappings/saddlebags) full of folds (i.e., packed with folded goods/trappings).
segments.s149-g73: 'چو' (= when) introduces a temporal clause; the verb 'دید' (saw) is past, and the object 'سهراب' is marked by the object marker 'را'. The prepositional phrase 'با یال و شاخ' (with mane and horn) follows the object to describe Sohrab's appearance.
segments.s149-g74: This clause describes an action toward Sohrab. 'چون' = 'like/as', 'بر' = 'against/toward', 'سام' is the proper name, 'جنگی' functions as an adjective/noun 'warlike / fighter', and 'فراخ' is an epithet 'broad/ample' (poetic praise). The sequence expresses a simile of the attack: '(he) upon him as upon Sam, a great warrior.' Note: 'برش' here functions as 'upon/against him' (poetic contraction).
segments.s149-g75: 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative) introduces the indirect object; 'گفت' is the reporting verb (said). 'از ایدر' = 'from here' (poetic 'ایدر' = here). 'به یکسو شویم' uses 'به' + directional noun 'یکسو' ('to one side') and the present/subjunctive form 'شویم' ('let us go / let us become'), giving a hortative meaning 'let us move aside.' Word order places the indirect object and reporting verb before the quoted/hortatory clause.
segments.s149-g76: 'به آوردگه' = 'to the arena/venue' (directional preposition + place noun). 'هر دو' = 'both of us/two', and 'همرو شویم' literally 'become companions/together' — here idiomatically 'we will go together.' 'شویم' is first-person-plural subjunctive/present used as a future/hortative after the proposal.
segments.s149-g77: Verb-subject inversion for poetic effect: 'بمالید' (he rubbed) is a past tense verb, followed by the subject 'سهراب'. 'کف را' is a definite direct object ('the palm'), marked by 'را', and 'به کف' ('into/against palm') completes the idiom 'کف را به کف' = 'palm to palm' (i.e., rubbing hands together).
segments.s149-g78: 'به آوردگه رفت' = 'he went to the arena' (directional 'به' + place + past 'رفت'). The final phrase 'از پیش صف' is ablative/positional: 'from before the ranks' meaning 'stepping forth in front of the battle-line'; 'از' = from, 'پیش صف' = 'before/in front of the line.'
segments.s149-g79: 'به رستم' = 'to Rostam' (dative). 'چنین گفت' = 'said thus' (reporting formula). 'کاندر' is an archaic relative/connector meaning 'that in which' or simply 'that', introducing the subordinate clause; 'گذشت' is past 'passed' or 'has passed.' The clause as a whole sets up the statement that follows.
segments.s149-g80: 'ز من' = 'from me' (archaic poetic 'ز' = از). 'جنگ و پیکار' are two synonyms 'war and battle/fighting' paired for emphasis. 'سوی تو گشت' = 'turned toward you' (سوی = toward, 'تو' = you, 'گشت' = became/turned). The clause states that the fighting which was previously with/around the speaker has shifted to Rostam.
segments.s149-g9: خروشید is a past, 3rd-person singular intransitive verb (poetic form of خروشیدن) meaning “he roared.” The conjunction و links that clause to the next; بگرفت is a past 3rd-person verb (he seized/took) with the b‑ prefix as part of the past form. نیزه is the direct object (“spear”), and به دست is a prepositional phrase literally ‘to/at hand’ used idiomatically here as ‘in his hand.’ Word order places the verb early for vivid narration and then the object with its locative phrase.
segments.s149-g10: به introduces the destination and takes آوردگه (“place of arrival/arena/venue”) as its complement; رفت is the past 3rd-person verb “he went.” The simile begins with چون (“like/as”) and is followed by پیل مست, a noun + adjective pair where مست is figurative (‘maddened/intoxicated’) modifying پیل (‘elephant’). The simile follows the motion verb to describe the manner of his advance.
segments.s149-g11: کس here carries the negative sense ‘no one.’ It is followed by از plus a genitive group (نامداران ایران سپاه) that identifies the set — ‘from the famed ones/host of Iran.’ سپاه functions as the noun ‘host/army’ modified by نامداران ایران; the whole is an oblique nominal phrase meaning ‘no one among the famed warriors of Iran.’
segments.s149-g12: نیارست is an older/poetic verbal form expressing inability or failure (negative/resultative nuance) and here serves as the main verbal idea ‘did not manage/was not able.’ کردن follows as a light verb forming the verbal construction. بدو is a dative/prepositional clitic meaning ‘to him/against him,’ and در نگاه is a locative phrase ‘in sight/in his presence.’ Together the phrase says that none could engage / stand up to him before his eyes.
segments.s149-g13: This is an enumerative phrase using the poetic preposition ز (variant of از) repeated for emphasis: ز پای و رکیب و ز دست و عنان lists paired items (‘from foot and rider’ and ‘from hand and reins’). The conjunctions و join the paired nouns; the construction enumerates things affected or cast down in parallel fashion.
segments.s149-g14: Again beginning with ز ‘from,’ بازوی is a genitive/possessive form ‘(his) arm.’ وز here is an archaic linker/particle (in this line functioning like ‘and/with’ or evoking weight/force in classical diction). آب‌داده is a compound adjective placed before the noun (literally ‘water‑given’ but used poetically for ‘moistened/soaked’), modifying سنان (‘spear’). The phrase groups arm and the (blood/ water‑)soaked spear as further victims/elements in the list.
segments.s149-g15: ازان پس is a temporal phrase ‘after that/thereupon.’ دلیران (the brave) is the subject and شدند (past of شدن) here indicates they ‘became’ or more idiomatically ‘gathered’ — followed by انجمن ‘an assembly/meeting,’ so the clause means ‘after that the brave gathered into council.’ This is typical narrative sequence phrasing in epic Persian.
segments.s149-g16: بگفتند is the reporting verb ‘they said.’ What follows is the reported address/invocation: کاینت is a demonstrative/vocative element (classical usage equivalent to ‘O you/this’ or ‘that which’ depending on context), گو is an imperative/verb root meaning ‘say/speak’ (here functioning as a command or exhortation), and پیلتن is a proper name or place. The phrase therefore records the assembly addressing Pilten with an imperative utterance; the exact force (‘say’ vs. ‘go’) is ambiguous in archaic usage but context favors an address to Pilten to speak or act.
segments.s149-g65: 'به دل گفت' is an idiom meaning 'he said to his heart' = 'he thought to himself'; 'گفت' is a past tense verb taking the following clause as its complement without an explicit conjunction (classical/poetic omission of که). In 'کین کار آهرمنست' کین (a noun) qualifies کار as 'a vengeful/hostile deed'; آهرمنست is a copular predicate (آهرمن + -ست = 'is Ahriman's / belongs to Ahriman'), so the whole clause means 'this vengeful deed is Ahriman's.'
segments.s149-g66: نه negates the following clause. 'این رستخیز' is the subject ('this uprising/resurrection'); 'از پی' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on account of / because of / following' (a classical collocation 'از پیِ ...' = 'because of ...'); 'یک تن' = 'one person'; 'تنست' is a predicate with the copular sense (poetic/archaic ending -ست) translating idiomatically as 'it is (just) of one man' → here meaning 'it is not (just) about one man.' The clause therefore means 'this uprising is not because of a single man.'
segments.s149-g67: Two coordinated past actions share the same subject: 'بزد دست' literally 'he struck (his) hand' (a gesture of emphasis). 'پوشید' is a past verb 'he put on / donned.' The phrase 'ببر بیان' is a poetic compound where ببر (tiger) metaphorically modifies بیان (eloquence/expression); the ezāfe is omitted in poetry, so 'پوشید ببر بیان' means 'he donned the tiger of eloquence' — i.e., assumed a fierce, eloquent tone.
segments.s149-g68: ببست is a past verb 'he bound/tied' (here 'he girded'). 'آن کیانی کمر' = 'that Kayanian belt' (آن + adjective/epithet کیانی modifying کمر); 'بر میان' = 'around the middle' (بر = on/upon, میان = middle). Together: 'he girded that royal/Kayanian belt about his middle.'
segments.s149-g69: نشست here is a past verb 'he sat / alighted'; the idiom 'نشست از بر رخش' = 'he dismounted from (on) Rakhsh' where از بر = 'from upon' and رخش is the steed's name. 'و بگرفت راه' = 'and he took the road' (بگرفت = 'he took/seized', راه = 'road / way'), i.e. 'he set out on the road.'
segments.s149-g70: This is a nominal apposition: 'زواره' (here a proper name) followed by its role 'نگهبان گاه و سپاه' = 'guardian/keeper of the place and of the host (army).' Poetic style omits the ezāfe, so the three nouns form 'Zowāreh, guardian of place and army.' It stands as an identifying phrase tied to the narrative continuation.
segments.s149-g71: درفشش (درفش + possessive suffix) = 'his banner/standard.' ببردند is a past plural 'they carried/bore' (poetic past). 'با او' = 'with him / along with him.' 'بهم' (= به‌هم) = 'together.' The whole unit: 'They bore his banner with him, together.'
segments.s149-g72: همی is a classical/poetic particle placed before past verbs to mark continuous or iterative action; 'همی رفت' = 'they/it went on / proceeded.' The adjectives 'پرخاشجوی و دژم' follow the verb and describe the subject (the force/men): 'aggressive and sullen/grim.' The subject is implicit (those who bore the banner / the host).
segments.s149-g57: ز خیمه is a prepositional phrase 'from the tent' that precedes the verbal phrase; نگه (a noun 'gaze' used as verbal noun) + کرد (past auxiliary) form the verbal idea 'looked'. رستم is the subject placed after the verb, following the flexible, often inverted word order of epic Persian. بدشت is an adverbial/directional phrase 'to/onto the plain' at the end.
segments.s149-g58: ز ره is another prepositional phrase 'from the road'; گیو را is the definite direct object marked by را (here the person seen); دید is the simple past 'saw'. کاندر گذشت is a compressed relative clause: کاندر = که + اندر ('who in/through it') and گذشت is 'passed', so together 'who was passing through it.'
segments.s149-g59: نهاد here is the past verb 'placed/put' (past of نهادن). از بر is an archaic double prepositional sequence meaning 'upon/on'; رخش is the steed and رخشنده is an epithet 'the radiant' (appositive or adjectival), and زین is the object 'saddle' that was placed. The verb precedes the phrase that specifies where/onto whom the saddle was put, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s149-g60: همی گفت is a habitual/continuative or narrative-telegraphic phrase often rendered 'then/kept saying' (here introducing speech). گرگین (Gorgin) follows as the addressee/name. که introduces the content of speech ('that'/'that is'), here leading into the quoted imperative بشتاب 'hurry!'; هین is an interjection/vocative (an exclamatory 'hey' / 'halt' depending on context) used to call attention.
segments.s149-g61: همی بست is 'he fastened/secured' (past). بر marks the target, باره is 'load/pack' (the thing fastened), and رهام is the proper name (Roham) in genitive relation; تنگ follows as an adjective/adverb meaning 'tight' or 'tightly', modifying the verb phrase. The element order (verb — object phrase — postpositive modifier) is typical in epic diction.
segments.s149-g62: به introduces a directional/target phrase ('to/on/at'); برگستوان is a bound lexical item here (given lemma: برگستوان) functioning as the target ('arm/forearm' or similar in context). بر زده is a compound where زده is the verbal participle/past 'struck' and the prefixed بر gives the sense 'struck upon/at'; طوس is the agent 'Tūs' and چنگ ('hand/claw') names the instrument. Poetic inversion places the target phrase before the verb and the agent after it.
segments.s149-g63: همی in narrative here marks the continuative/episodic action. این بدان آن بدین is a classical poetic formula meaning 'this to that, that to this' (i.e. they exchanged remarks); the written splits into ب + دان and ب + دین are the prepositional prefix 'بِ' (to) attached to forms that in context function as directional/reciprocal markers (بدان, بدین). گفت زود places the verb 'said' with the adverb زود 'quickly' following it. Note: the database senses for دان and دین do not correspond to their bound use in the fixed phrases 'بدان' and 'بدین', so their sense index is marked as unavailable.
segments.s149-g64: تهمتن is an epithet 'the mighty one' (used as subject). چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از خیمه آوا is a prepositional phrase 'a voice from the tent' (آوا = sound/voice). شنود is the third-person verb 'he heard' (an older/epic form equivalent to modern شنید), completing the temporal clause 'when the mighty one heard the voice from the tent.'
segments.s149-g105: چنین (thus) + داد پاسخ (gave an answer) is a fixed poetic order meaning "he replied thus." که introduces the reported speech that follows. رستم نیم is an archaic/poetic negated copula: نیم here functions like modern نیستم, i.e. "I am not Rostam," with نیم marking first‑person negative rather than the literal dictionary sense "half."
segments.s149-g106: هم ... نیم continues the negative statement in parallel: هم (here "also/too") + از تخمه سام (a prepositional phrase, "from the seed/lineage of Sam") followed by another instance of the archaic negative copula, producing the sense "nor am I also of Sam's line." نیرم in the middle does not match the supplied adjectival senses and functions as part of the same verbal/elliptical construction in this poetic context.
segments.s149-g107: که here gives a reason/explanation: "for" / "that." او پهلوانست is a third‑person statement (پهلوان + است) meaning "he is a hero/champion." و links clauses, and من کهترم is first person with a predicative adjective (کهتر = "inferior/smaller") with the suffix -م "I am," giving "and I am lesser."
segments.s149-g108: نه ... نه is a standard "neither ... nor" construction. با تخت means "with a throne" (تخت = throne). گاهم is a possessive/qualifying form (گاه + ـم) functioning here within the list of regalia/seat‑terms and is best read as part of the clausal negation. با افسرم means "with my afsar/helmet/crown" (افسر here = helmet/crest), so the whole clause says he has neither throne nor helmet (i.e. no royal accoutrements).
segments.s149-g109: از امید سهراب is a prepositional phrase meaning "from/because of hope in Sohrab" (i.e. hope placed on Sohrab). شد is the past of شدن meaning "became," and ناامید is the predicate adjective "hopeless/disappointed;" together شد ناامید = "became hopeless/disappointed."
segments.s149-g110: برو here is a short narrative particle meaning roughly "thereupon/then" (not the literal imperative "go"), followed by تیره شد = "turned/darkened" (تیره = dark, شد = became). روی روز سپید is a poetic genitive/attributive chain: روی (face) qualified by روز سپید ("white/bright as day"), so the whole phrase means "his once bright/white face grew dark."
segments.s149-g1: چو is a temporal subordinating particle 'when'; بشنید is 3rd-person past 'he heard' (بـ- prefix here is not present; this is simply the past). این گفتهای درشت is the noun phrase 'these harsh words' (گفتهای = 'words said' / گفت‌ها in modern spelling, درشت = 'harsh/rude'). The whole clause sets the time for the following main clause.
segments.s149-g2: نهان کرد is the compound verb 'hid' (نهان + کرد, with کرد as light verb); ازو is the prepositional source 'from him'; روی 'face' is the direct object of نهان کرد. و links the second coordinated verb بنمود (an older/poetic past of نمودن) 'showed' and پشت 'back' is its object. Word order is typical Persian: verb then object(s) or, here, verb + prepositional phrase + conjunction + verb + object.
segments.s149-g3: ز بالا is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from above'. زدش is past 'he hit/struck him' with the enclitic object -ش = 'him'. تند is an adverb 'swiftly/forcefully'. یک modifies the following nominal phrase پشت دست 'the back of the hand' (i.e. 'one backhand/blow'). The sequence gives manner and instrument of the blow.
segments.s149-g4: بیفگند is an older past form 'he threw/flung (him)'; و is the coordinating 'and/then'; آمد is past 'he came'; به جای نشست is a fixed phrase 'sat in (his) place' — به (towards/into) + جای (place) + نشست (past of نشستن 'to sit, settle'). The verbs are sequential actions: threw, came, sat.
segments.s149-g5: بپوشید is past 3rd-person 'he put on / donned'. خفتان here functions as the item put on (context suggests a cloak or outer garment); because the provided sense list does not include that meaning, the exact lexical sense is marked below as unmatched. و is 'and'; بر سر نهاد is a verb phrase 'placed (it) upon (his) head' (نهاد is past of نهادن 'to put').
segments.s149-g6: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one'; خود is emphatic 'himself' or 'himself personally'; چینی here denotes 'a Chinese (person)'; به کردار is a prepositional simile-former 'in the manner of' (به + کردار 'conduct/way'); باد is the simile 'wind' — together 'a certain Chinese himself like the wind' (i.e. moved/sprung like the wind). The verb of motion is implied from context.
segments.s149-g7: ز تندی is a causal phrase 'because of the swiftness/violence'. به جوش آمدش is an idiom 'came to a boil' where آمدش = 'came to him/it' (the enclitic -ش refers back to the subject, here خون); خون is 'blood'. برگ here is uncertain in isolation (not the usual sense 'leaf') and is treated as lexically unclear in the senses below; syntactically the clause says 'because of the violence his blood came to a boil.'
segments.s149-g8: نشست is past 'settled' (here the blood is the understood subject); از بر functions together as a locative relation 'upon / on' in this older word order; باره + تیزتگ names the place/item where it settled — تیزتگ is 'sharp blade/point'. باره's exact nuance here is unclear in the provided senses and so is left unspecified below. Overall: 'it settled on the (edge/point) of the sharp blade.'
segments.s149-g25: تنت را is the definite, object-marked phrase 'your body' (تنت + object marker را). برین نیزه is a locative demonstrative phrase 'upon this spear' (بر = 'on/upon', ین = archaic/poetic variant of 'این' = 'this'). بریان کنم is the verb phrase 'I will roast' or 'I will cause to be roasted' with کنم as the 1st-person singular present/future-form of کردن used to express intent or causation. Word order is object + locative + verb, typical of Persian SOV in poetry.
segments.s149-g26: ستاره is the noun 'star' (here a celestial/star image). بدین is the fused preposition+demonstrative ('به این' = 'because of/at this' or 'by this'); the data split it as ب + دین, but functionally it means 'at/for this'. کار is 'deed/act', گریان is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'making (one) weep' or 'causing to weep', and کنم (from کردن) is 'I will cause' — so the phrase says 'I will cause the star to weep at this deed.'
segments.s149-g27: یکی سخت سوگند is a noun phrase 'a certain/one strong oath' or 'a vehement oath' (یکی = 'a certain/one', سخت = 'severe/firmly' modifying سوگند = 'oath'). خوردم is the verb from خوردن used idiomatically with سوگند to mean 'I took/swear an oath' (classical usage); به بزم is a prepositional phrase 'at the feast/banquet'. The clause thus states the speaker's action of taking a strong oath at the banquet.
segments.s149-g28: بدان شب = 'on that night' (بدان is a fused preposition+demonstrative 'به + آن' in classical/poetic Persian), کجا introduces a relative/interrogative 'where', کشته شد is a passive construction 'was killed' (شد here as auxiliary 'was'), and ژنده‌رزم is the noun 'the ragged warrior' (a personal epithet). Together: 'on that night where the ragged warrior was slain.'
segments.s149-g29: کز ایران نمانم is a clause meaning 'that from Iran I will leave/there will remain none' (کز = 'that/from which', ایران = 'Iran', نمانم = 'I will not leave/remain' or 'shall not remain'). یکی نیزه‌دار = 'a single spearman' (یکی = 'one/not a single', نیزه‌دار = 'spear‑bearer/spearman'). The overall sense is a threat to leave not one spearman in Iran.
segments.s149-g30: کنم is 'I will do/bring about'. زنده کاووس = 'Kavus alive' (زنده qualifying کاووس). کی را: کی here functions as the interrogative/relative 'who' in the manuscript split, but in context it helps mark the object (کاووس را); را is the definite object marker, به دار = 'to the gallows' (دار = 'gallows') — together: 'I will cause/have Kavus hung alive on the gallows.' The verb is placed at the start (poetic inversion) but governs the whole object phrase.
segments.s149-g31: که here is a relative conjunction 'that/who'. داری is 2nd-person singular 'you have/possess'. از ایرانیان is a partitive/location phrase 'from/among the Iranians'. تیز is an adjective 'sharp/keen' modifying چنگ, and چنگ (literally 'claw' or 'talon') is used metaphorically for a fierce fighter or a sharp hand. The clause says 'who has among the Iranians a keen claw.'
segments.s149-g32: که again introduces a relative clause 'who'. پیش من آید = 'will come before me' (پیش = 'before/in front of', من = 'me', آید = 3rd-person singular present/future 'comes/will come'). به هنگام جنگ is a temporal phrase 'at the time of battle' (به هنگام = 'at the time of', جنگ = 'battle'). Together it qualifies the previous noun: 'who will come before me in the battle.'
segments.s149-g33: همی is an archaic continuous adverb meaning “kept/continually.” گفت is the lexical verb “said” but here is used in the continuous narrative frame همی گفت. و links the clauses. می‌بود is the imperfective past copula (“was”) describing a continuing state, followed by the predicative adjective جوشان “seething/boiling,” and بسی intensifies it (‘very/much’). Together they mean “he kept saying and was very agitated.”
segments.s149-g34: از ایران is a prepositional phrase marking source/group (“from among the Iranians”). ندادند is the 3rd-person plural past negative of دادن (“they did not give”); here it governs the direct object پاسخ “answer.” کسی in this negative context means ‘no one’ (indefinite negative). Word order is somewhat archaic: verb before its indefinite subject.
segments.s149-g35: خروشان is an adjectival/adverbial participle meaning “roaring/tumultuous,” giving the manner of arrival. بیامد is the simple past “he came.” به + پرده‌سرای is the prepositional phrase “into the curtain‑hall/pavilion”; the whole phrase reads “he came roaring into the pavilion.”
segments.s149-g36: This clause has an archaic word order: به نیزه (marking the spear as the thing concerned) + درآورد (past verb “drew out/produced”) + بالا (adverb “up”) + ز جای (“from its place”). Literally: “to the spear he brought up from the place,” i.e. he drew the spear up out of its socket. ز is the poetic form of از (“from”).
segments.s149-g37: خم آورد is an idiomatic past expression “he bent” (lit. ‘brought a bend’). زان پس = “after that/thereupon.” سنان here is the spear‑point (not a personal name) and کرد is the causative/main verb “made/did,” with سیخ “a pointed stake/skewer” as the result: “thereafter he made the spear‑point into a spike.”
segments.s149-g38: بزد is the simple past “he struck.” The following phrase نیزه برکند is verbal-compositional: برکند (“dislodged/uprooted”) applies to the object هفتاد میخ (“seventy pegs”), i.e. by striking with the spear he uprooted seventy pegs. The numeral + noun is the direct object placed after the verb cluster.
segments.s149-g39: سراپرده is the inner curtain/inner part of the pavilion. یک بهره = “one portion/part.” آمد is the past “came” used here intransitively to mean ‘came away/fell.’ ز پای literally “from the foot/base” means ‘from its fastening at the bottom.’ The phrase means ‘a part of the inner curtain came loose from its lower fastening.’
segments.s149-g40: ز هر سو = “from every side.” برآمد is the past “arose/sounded forth.” دم کرنای is a genitive construction “the blast (دم) of the trumpet (کرنای),” so the whole segment means “from every side the trumpet‑blasts rose.”
segments.s149-g17: نشاید is an impersonal negative judgment ('it is not fitting/one should not') taking a following verbal-noun clause. نگه کردن is a compound verbal-noun + light verb ('to keep/watch'); here نگه (watch, keeping) is the substantive and کردن is the light verb. آسان modifies نگه کردن ('easily' / 'with ease'). بدوی is an adjectival/poetic modifier attached to the phrase (an older/formal word used as an intensifier or qualifier of manner).
segments.s149-g18: که introduces the subordinate (explanatory or causal) clause linked to the previous judgment. یارد شدن is the compound of noun + شدن ('to become a companion/helper'); پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before him', with پیش marking location/position relative to او. جنگجوی is the subject/agent of شدن placed at the end for emphasis ('a warrior').
segments.s149-g19: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial ('thereupon/after that'). خروشید is the simple past (3rd singular) of خروشیدن ('he roared, cried out') with سهراب as its subject. گرد is a postposed adverb meaning 'around' or 'about' and follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s149-g20: همی is a continuative/emphatic adverb here ('then/indeed/at once') modifying the action that follows. شاه کاووس را is the definite object marked by را ('King Kavus' as the addressee/object). بر + شمرد is a verbal compound: بر as a prefixed preposition/particle ('toward/at') combines with شمرد (past of شمر/شمردن 'to count/announce/call out') — together meaning 'called out to / addressed'. Note the object (شاه کاووس را) precedes the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s149-g21: چنین گفت is a formulaic phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here is the past verb 'said'); با introduces the indirect object/recipient ('to' or 'with' the king). شاه is 'king' (the addressee) and آزاد مرد is the subject noun phrase 'the noble/free man' (آزاد here = 'noble/generous', مرد = 'man/hero'). Word order is verb + prepositional object + subject (poetic inversion).
segments.s149-g22: که introduces the indirect question/contents of the speech. چون است = 'how is' (چون as the interrogative/adverb 'how', است = 'is'). کارت is the possessed noun 'your plan/your action' (کار + ـت). به دشت نبرد is a locative/phrase meaning 'in/for the plain of battle' (به marking direction/location, دشت = 'plain', نبرد = 'battle' forming the compound 'battle-plain' or 'battlefield'). The overall structure is an indirect question: 'how is your plan in the plain of battle?'.
segments.s149-g23: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. کرده‌ای is the perfect/past participle + auxiliary (کرده + ـای) forming a 2nd-person perfect: 'you have done / you have taken'. نام کاووس is the object 'the name Kavus' (i.e., 'taken the name Kavus'). کی at the end is an interrogative 'who (are you)?' placed after the clause for force/emphasis.
segments.s149-g24: که here is causal/explanatory ('for/because'). در جنگ is locative 'in war/battle'. نه ... نه ... is a paired negation ('neither ... nor ...'). تاو داری means 'you have radiance/glow' (تاو = radiance, داری = 'you have'), and پی is 'following / followers / pursuit' (so نه پی = 'no followers/support'). The clause explains/censures the addressee's lack of qualities in battle.
segments.s149-g89: بدو گفت = ‘to him said’ — a common Classical Persian order where the indirect object ('بدو' = “to him”) precedes the verb 'گفت' (past ‘said’). نرم‌ای is a vocative/imperative: نرم (be gentle/soft) with the vocative particle ای; جوان‌مرد گرم is the addressed noun phrase (جوان‌مرد “chivalrous/young‑man”) followed by the adjective گرم (“ardent/zealous”), attributive after the noun as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s149-g90: This is a pair of coordinated noun+adjective phrases with no copula: زمین سرد و خشک ‘the earth (is) cold and dry’ and سخن گرم و نرم ‘speech (is) warm and gentle’. Adjectives follow their nouns and are linked by و; the clause presents a contrast between the harsh ground and the desirable tone of speech.
segments.s149-g91: به پیری = a temporal prepositional phrase ‘in old age’ (به + پیری). بسی is an adverbial 'much/many' modifying the verb; دیدم is first‑person past ‘I saw’; آوردگاه is the object 'battlefield(s)'. Word order is flexible in poetry so the temporal phrase and quantifier precede the verb.
segments.s149-g92: بسی (again: 'many/much') opens the line; بر زمین پست is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'on the low/abject ground' (بر = upon, زمین = ground, پست = low). کردم سپاه = 'I made/caused (an) army' —کردم is first‑person past 'I did/caused' and سپاه is its object; taken together: 'I laid many an army low on the ground.' The poetic order places the object after the verb and the locative phrase before it.
segments.s149-g93: تبه شد is a past intransitive/resultative ‘was ruined/was undone’ or ‘perished’; بسی quantifies (many). دیو is the subject 'demon(s)' and در جنگ من is a locative/genitive phrase 'in my battle' ('من' possessive after جنگ). The clause means 'many demons were ruined in my fight.'
segments.s149-g94: ندیدم = 'I did not see' (negated past). بدان سو is a locative phrase 'to/at that side/direction' (بدان = به + آن). که بودم شکن is a relative clause 'where I was a breaker' (که = ‘that/where’, بودم = 'I was' past, شکن = 'breaker/one who breaks'); together: 'I did not see on that side where I stood anyone who could break me.'
segments.s149-g95: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (watch/keep guard) + کن (do) — 'keep watch'. مرا‌گر ببینی is a conditional subordinate 'if you see me' where مرا (me) precedes گر (archaic conditional particle 'if') and ببینی is 'you see' (subjunctive/present); به جنگ is the locative 'in battle'. The conditional clause is embedded after the imperative.
segments.s149-g96: اگر introduces the condition 'if'. زنده مانی uses زنده 'alive' plus the archaic/poetic 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form مانی (you remain/stay) — 'if you remain alive'. مترس is the negative imperative 'fear not' (do not be afraid). از نهنگ = 'of/from the whale' (از = of/from; نهنگ = whale, here a metaphor for a great monster/enemy).
segments.s149-g41: رمید is a past simple verb (he/it fled); in classical/poetic Persian the verb often appears before its subject. آن دلاور سپاه دلیر is a noun phrase identifying the one who fled: آن (demonstrative 'that/the') + دلاور (noun 'hero/valiant one') with سپاه دلیر ('the brave host') as appositional/specifying phrase — together: 'that hero of the brave host.'
segments.s149-g42: به کردار گوران is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/behavior of the Gūrān' (کردار = 'manner/conduct'); here به functions as the preposition introducing the manner. ز چنگال شیر is another prepositional phrase 'from the lion's claw(s)', with ز = 'from' and چنگال شیر = 'lion's talon(s)'. Both phrases attach to رمید (he fled): 'he fled in the manner of the Gūrān, from the lion's claws.'
segments.s149-g43: غمی گشت is a predicative phrase 'became sorrowful' (غمی = 'sorrow/grief', گشت = past of شدن 'became'). The name کاووس follows as the subject (common in epic/poetic inversion). و آواز داد joins the action: آواز داد = 'he gave a cry / uttered a call' where داد is the light verb 'gave' combining with آواز.
segments.s149-g44: کزین here functions as 'from these / among these' introducing a subset. نامداران فرخ نژاد is a noun phrase: نامداران = 'the famed/renowned ones' modified postpositively by the epithet فرخ نژاد ('fortunate/noble‑born'); together: 'these renowned, noble‑born men' — the phrase supplies the referents of what he cried out about.
segments.s149-g45: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one / someone / a man'. نزد + proper name (نزدِ رستم) marks 'to Rostam' or 'in Rostam's presence'. برید is a past verbal form meaning 'carried / brought' (classical usage) and آگهی is the object 'news/information' — so: 'someone came to Rostam and brought news.'
segments.s149-g46: This clause reports the content of the news. کزین ('from/ because of these') introduces the cause/agent (these Turks). ترَک is the noun 'Turk(s)' here. شد + تهی is a verbal predicate 'became empty' (شد = became, تهی = empty). مغز گردان ('the brains of the troop(s)') is the affected noun phrase; the literary word order places the cause (کزین ترک) before the predicate — i.e. 'by these Turks the troops' brains became empty.'
segments.s149-g47: ندارم is the negative present (or presentive) 'I do not have' (دارم = I have; here negated). سواری is the noun 'rider/horseman' as direct object. ورا هم‌نبرد is a qualifying phrase modifying سواری: ورا ('to/for him' = 'for that man') + هم‌نبرد ('equal in fight / comrade‑in‑arms') — together 'a horseman equal to him in battle.' Word order places the negated verb before its object, as is common.
segments.s149-g48: This clause states inability among the Iranians. از ایران = 'from Iran / among the Iranians'. The following word نیارد appears here in an archaic/poetic form that functions in context like a negative/irrealis verbal element (the exact classical form is irregular; see note), after which کس means 'no one' and این کار کرد = 'did this deed' (کرد = past 'did'). The overall sense: 'no one from Iran has done this deed.'
segments.s149-g97: مرا دید = 'he saw me.' The object (مرا) comes before the verb (دید), which is normal in Persian. در جنگ دریا و کوه is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in the battle of sea and mountain' (در + noun); در introduces the locative 'in/at.'
segments.s149-g98: که introduces a relative/adverbial clause ('where' / 'in which') modifying the preceding battle. با + NP marks accompaniment ('with'); نامداران توران is a genitive phrase 'the famed ones of Turan' (نامداران modified by the place-name توران), and گروه is the head noun 'a group / band / troop.' Word order is typical (preposition + modifiers + head noun).
segments.s149-g99: چه کردم is an exclamatory/interrogative phrasing 'what I have done' (چه + past verb کردم). ستاره is the subject 'the star' (here functioning as fate/fortune), گوای is predicate/participial here meaning 'bearing witness / declaring', and منست is an archaic/copular-like form linking the clause (here roughly 'is mine / is manifest to me' — an old poetic copula).
segments.s149-g100: به مردی is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to/for a man' (به + indefinite مردی). جهان زیر پای is a possessive-locative construction 'the world under (his) foot/feet' where زیر is the preposition 'under' and پای is the noun 'foot.' The final منست again functions as an archaic copular/predicative element tying the idea together ('is / belongs').
segments.s149-g101: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it', followed by گفت 'said'). کز is an older form of که از or که 'that / from', here introducing the content clause 'that I may ask you' or 'that from you I should ask.' بپرسم is the 1st-person subjunctive/present (ب + پرس stem + م) meaning 'I ask / I should ask' and functions as the verb of the subordinate clause; سخن is the object 'word(s)/the matter.'
segments.s149-g102: همه راستی = 'all truth' (همه modifies راستی). باید is the modal 'must / ought to' and governs the following verb. افگند is the root meaning 'to cast / throw' used here in a poetic/elliptical sense 'to cast forth / lay out' (i.e. 'to state / utter'); بن is literal 'base/under' but in this collocation functions idiomatically with افگند ('cast down / put forth') — overall: 'all truth must be put forth / told.'
segments.s149-g103: من ایدون گمانم = 'I now suppose / I suppose for the moment.' من is the first-person pronoun. ایدون is an older/poetic form meaning 'now / at present / appearing' (verb-derived adverbial), and گمانم is 'my belief / I suppose' (گمان + enclitic -م 'my/I'). که تو رستمی is the content clause 'that you are Rostam', with رستمی a predicative adjective/noun meaning 'Rostam-like / of Rostam.'
segments.s149-g104: گر = conditional 'if.' از تخمه نامور = 'from a famed seed/offspring' (از + تخمه 'offspring/stock' + اسم‌گذاریِ منسوب نامور 'renowned'). نیرمی at the end is an adjectival/epithet form; in older Persian it may indicate a quality ('strong/able') or refer to a name/epithet — the precise meaning is ambiguous here and depends on older lexical usage, so it functions as the predicate of the conditional 'if you are of famed stock (نیرمی).'
segments.s149-g81: This clause says there will be no other companion for you in Iran. نخواهی is the 2nd-person form of خواستن used here with negation (ن-) to mean 'you will not (need/find/want)'; دگر یار کس = 'another companion/anyone else' (دگر modifies یار). Word order is typical older Persian: prepositional phrase 'از ایران' ('from/in Iran') comes first for emphasis.
segments.s149-g82: چو ... باشم introduces a conditional 'if I am (with you)'. چو here = 'if'; باشم is 1st-person subjunctive/present 'I be' (classical poetic form). بآورد بس is a short predicative phrase: بآورد (3rd‑person verb 'it/that will bring/produce') + بس 'enough' → 'that will suffice.' The sequence links the condition ('if I am with you') to the result ('that will be enough').
segments.s149-g83: به آوردگه 'at/to the gathering-place/arena' uses به as a locative preposition; آوردگه = 'place/venue'. بر ت(را) (here written separately as بر + ت + را) means 'for you' or 'on you' — the enclitic ت marks the 2nd person object and را is the definite/object marker. جای نیست is an existential negative: 'there is no place/room.'
segments.s149-g84: ترا خود = 'you yourself' (ت + را split orthographically; خود is emphatic). به یک مشت من = 'against/compared to one handful of mine' (مشت = 'handful' here, i.e. a small force/amount). پای نیست is an idiomatic negation meaning 'has no standing' or 'is no match' — literally 'there is no foot/standing.' Together: 'you yourself have no standing even against one handful of mine.'
segments.s149-g85: This is a descriptive noun phrase: به بالا بلندی = 'of/with high loftiness' (به used to mark the attribute), و با کتف و یال = 'and with shoulder and mane' — a series of body/appearance features. The coordination (و ... و ...) lists the parts that make up the creature's proud appearance.
segments.s149-g86: This clause is passive/experiential: ستم یافت = 'suffered injustice/was oppressed' (یافت 'was found/was afflicted' here). بالت = 'your wing' (ب‑ + الت with the 2nd‑person suffix), ز بسیار سال = 'by many years' (ز = 'from/by', بسیار سال = 'many years' — i.e. long age/time has harmed the wing). The verb follows the noun phrase, a common classical order.
segments.s149-g87: نگه کرد رستم = 'Rostam looked' (نگه 'a look/gaze' + کرد 'did' — a common light-verb construction). In poetry the verb often precedes or stands next to the subject. بدان سرفراز = 'at/unto that proud one' (ب- + دان forming بدان 'to/at that', سرفراز is the adjectival label 'proud/august').
segments.s149-g88: بدان introduces the object of the gaze again 'at that'; then a coordinated list: چنگ (here 'claw/talon') و یال ('and mane') و رکیب دراز ('and the long rakīb' — رکیب in this context = 'companion/rider', دراز = 'long/tall'). The sequence enumerates features Rostam inspected. Word order and the connective و are normal coordinating devices in classical Persian.

sentences.s149-s25: Tūs rose and took Kāvus's message; having heard (it) he recounted the words before him.
sentences.s149-s26: Rostam said to him: ‘Any king who suddenly desires me...’
sentences.s149-s27: Sometimes you were treasure, sometimes the music of the feast; I saw from Kāvus nothing but the hardship of war.
sentences.s149-s28: He ordered that they saddle his steed; the riders who were to depart filled (it) full of folds.
sentences.s149-s37: When he saw Sohrab, with mane and horn, he charged at him like (he would) against Sam, a mighty warrior.
sentences.s149-s38: He said to him, 'From here let us move aside; to the arena we two will go together.'
sentences.s149-s39: Sohrab rubbed his palms together; he went to the arena, coming forward from the ranks.
sentences.s149-s40: He said to Rostam thus: 'That which was the war and combat with me has passed and turned toward you.'
sentences.s149-s5: He roared and seized the spear in his hand, and went to the place of encounter like a maddened elephant.
sentences.s149-s6: No one of the renowned men of Iran’s host could meet him in the field of view (i.e. stand before him).
sentences.s149-s7: From feet and riders, and from hands and reins, and from arm and the water‑soaked spear.
sentences.s149-s8: After that the brave assembled into council and said, 'O you — say (it), Pilten!'
sentences.s149-s33: He said to his heart (to himself), "This deed is Ahriman's; this uprising is not due to a single man."
sentences.s149-s34: He struck his hand and, putting on the tiger of eloquence, bound that Kayanian belt about his middle.
sentences.s149-s35: He dismounted from Rakhsh and took the road; Zowāreh, the guard of the place and the host (army)...
sentences.s149-s36: They bore his banner with him together; they advanced (or went) on, aggressive and grim.
sentences.s149-s29: Rostam looked from the tent toward the plain; he saw Giv on the road who was passing through it.
sentences.s149-s30: He put a shining saddle upon Rakhsh, then said to Gorgin: 'Make haste—hey!'
sentences.s149-s31: He fastened Roham's pack tightly, and Tus struck (at) the arm with his hand.
sentences.s149-s32: He quickly exchanged words this way and that; when the mighty one heard a voice from the tent, ...
sentences.s149-s53: He answered thus: "I am not Rostam; nor am I of the seed/lineage of Sam."
sentences.s149-s54: For he is a champion and I am inferior; neither with a throne nor with my helmet do I stand (i.e. I have no royal trappings).
sentences.s149-s55: Because of hope in Sohrab he became disheartened; then his face, once white as day, darkened.
sentences.s149-s1: When he heard those harsh words, he hid his face from him and showed his back.
sentences.s149-s2: From above he struck him swiftly with the back of his hand; he flung him down, then came and sat in his place.
sentences.s149-s3: He put on his cloak and placed it on his head; a certain Chinese man himself (moved) like the wind.
sentences.s149-s4: Because of the fierceness, his blood came to a boil; it settled upon the edge of the sharp blade.
sentences.s149-s13: I will roast your body upon this spear; I will make the star weep at this deed.
sentences.s149-s14: I took a solemn oath at the banquet that night where the ragged warrior was slain.
sentences.s149-s15: That none shall remain in Iran — not a single spearman; I will hang Kavus alive on the gallows.
sentences.s149-s16: For you possess among the Iranians a keen claw (i.e., a fierce fighter) who will come before me in the time of battle.
sentences.s149-s17: He kept speaking and was seething greatly; no one of the Iranians gave any answer.
sentences.s149-s18: He came in a roar into the curtain-hall and raised his spear up from its place.
sentences.s149-s19: He bent (it); then he made the spear‑point a spike — he struck with the spear and uprooted seventy pegs.
sentences.s149-s20: A portion of the inner curtain came away from the foot, and from every side the blast of the trumpets rose up.
sentences.s149-s9: It is not fitting to keep watch easily, for a warrior may become a companion before him.
sentences.s149-s10: Thereupon Sohrab roared about; he then turned to King Kavus and called out to him.
sentences.s149-s11: Thus spoke the noble man to the king: 'How is your plan for the battle on the plain?'
sentences.s149-s12: Why have you taken the name 'Kavus', who are you? For in war you have neither radiance nor followers.
sentences.s149-s45: He said to him, “Be gentle, O brave, warm-hearted man: the earth is cold and dry, and speech should be warm and gentle.”
sentences.s149-s46: In my old age I have seen many a battlefield; many times I have laid an army low upon the ground.
sentences.s149-s47: Many a demon was ruined in my battle; I saw no one on that side where I stood who was a breaker of me.
sentences.s149-s48: Keep watch over me if you see me in battle; if you remain alive, do not be afraid of the whale.
sentences.s149-s21: That valiant champion of the brave host fled in the manner of the Gūrān, away from the lion's claws.
sentences.s149-s22: Kavus was stricken with grief and cried out: 'From among these renowned, noble-born men...'
sentences.s149-s23: A man came to Rostam with news that, on account of these Turks, the troops' brains had been emptied.
sentences.s149-s24: I have no horseman equal to him in battle; no one from Iran has done this deed.
sentences.s149-s49: He saw me in the battle between sea and mountain, where a band of Turan's famed warriors stood.
sentences.s149-s50: What I have done my star proclaims; to a man, the world lies beneath his feet.
sentences.s149-s51: He said to him: 'I will ask you (about it); all truth must be told.'
sentences.s149-s52: For now I suppose that you are Rostam; if you spring from a famed stock, (you must be) powerful.
sentences.s149-s41: You will not find another companion in Iran; if I am with you, that will be enough.
sentences.s149-s42: There is no place for you at the gathering-place; you yourself are not a match even for one handful of mine.
sentences.s149-s43: Lofty in height, with shoulder and mane; your wing has been wronged by many years.
sentences.s149-s44: Rostam cast a glance at that proud one — at that claw and mane and the long companion (rider).
